votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using an iterative approach to sum even elements at odd indices, which is functionally equivalent to the recursive Coq definition.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq logic, using a loop to sum even elements at odd indices, which matches the recursive Coq function.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic using a loop to sum even elements at odd indices, and the pre/spec functions match exactly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic. Both iterate through the list, tracking the current index starting from 0. The condition `(curr_n % 2 != 0) and (h % 2 == 0)` in Python matches `andb (Nat.odd n) (Z.even h)` in Coq - both check if the index is odd and the element is even. The sum accumulation logic is identical. The precondition and specification also match their Coq counterparts.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic. Both iterate through the list, tracking the current index starting from 0. They check if the index is odd (n % 2 != 0 in Python, Nat.odd n in Coq) and if the element is even (h % 2 == 0 in Python, Z.even h in Coq), summing those elements. The precondition and specification match semantically.
Both implementations sum even elements at odd 0-based indices starting from n=0; parity checks match for all integers (including negatives), and pre/spec mirror Coq definitions.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it sums elements that are even and at odd indices starting from index 0; handling of evenness (including negatives) matches, and the precondition and spec checks return the same truth values.
